coredata.dc:title: Ionic liquid-enhanced lemon biomass carbon dots with sustainable use in bionic antibody microspheres for urea capture and ethyl carbamate inhibition
coredata.dc:creator: Meng, Chen
Xie, Chenchen
He, Jingbo
Chen, Xiaolin
Liu, Huilin
Sun, Baoguo
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we reported the room-temperature fabrication of ionic liquid-modified carbon dots encapsulated in bionic antibodies (IL-modified CDs@BAs) by one-pot green synthesis. In order to enhance the fluorescence intensity of CDs, imidazole ILs and lemon rich in heteroatoms were selected as CDs modifiers and sources. The resulting IL-modified CDs@BAs showed good selectivity and capture toward urea and obviously induced fluorescence quenching by template-binding. The inhibition rate ofIL-modified CDs@BAs on the urea pathway of ethyl carbamate was about 29.07% in the simulated Huangjiu system, indicating a good inhibitory effect. The IL-modified CDs@BAs system was also reproducible after five consecutive uses, thus reducing the economic cost. This research would expand the application fields of BAs-based optical sensing system from the perspectives of energy conservation, environmental protection and resource recovery, focusing on their application in the field of food safety control.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ionic liquid
Lemon
Biomass carbon dots
Bionic antibody
Ethyl carbamate
Urea

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of lemon biomass carbon dots (LBCDs)
Synthesis of IL-modified CDs@BAs and its control group
Selectivity, interference, and competitiveness analysis of IL-modified CDs@BAs
Evaluation of fluorescence properties of LBCDs
Stability and reusability analysis of LBCDs and IL-modified CDs@BAs
Capture of urea in real samples and inhibition of EC in simulated systems
Synthesis of IL-modified CDs@BAs and its urea capture mechanism
Effect of ILs on IL-modified CDs@ BAs and fluorescent detection kinetics
Fluorescence response-concentration dependence for IL-modified CDs@BAs for urea
Selectivity, competitiveness, and interference analysis of IL-modified CDs@BAs
Capture of urea in real samples and inhibition of EC in simulated systems

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation process of LBCDs (A) and IL-modified CDs@BAs (B). (C) The main bonding reactions in the preparation of IL-modified CDs@BAs. (D) UV–vis spectrum of LBCDs (a), urea (b), LBCDs with urea (c) and the optimal emission spectrum of LBCDs (d). (E) Transient fluorescence spectrum of LBCDs. (F) Transient fluorescence spectrum of LBCDs mixed with urea.

(A) TEM image of LBCDs with a scale bar of 10 nm (inset: size distribution of LBCDs). (B) SEM image of IL-modified CDs@ BAs with a scale bar of 200 nm (inset: size distribution of IL-modified CDs@ BAs). (C) TEM image of IL-modified CDs@ BAs with a scale bar of 200 nm. (D) EDS spectrum of IL-modified CDs@ BAs.

(A) Fluorescence responses of IL-modified CDs@IL-modified CDs@BAs in the presence of urea at various concentrations (0.01–5 μg L−1). (B) Linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of IL-modified CDs@IL-modified CDs@BAs and urea concentration. (C) Fluorescence responses of Control group in the presence of urea at various concentrations (0.01–5 μg L−1). (D) Linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of Control group and urea concentration.

(A) Effect of urea and its analogs on the fluorescence of IL-modified CDs@IL-modified CDs@BAs. (B) The quenching constant of IL-modified CDs@IL-modified CDs@BAs and Control group for urea and its analogs. (C) Competitive response of MIPs on IL-modified CDs@IL-modified CDs@BAs to urea and its analogs. (D) Fluorescence response of IL-modified CDs@IL-modified CDs@BAs to urea in the presence of potential interfering species. (E) 3D structural formula of urea and its analogues.

(A) GC–MS chromatogram of the relative strength of EC in simulation system 2 with 0 or 100 mg IL-modified CDs@BAs, respectively. (B) Effects of different contents of IL-modified CDs@BAs on EC resistance control in simulated Huangjiu. (C) Mechanism diagram of inhibiting EC formation by adding IL-modified CDs@BAs.
Capure and recoveries of the Huangjiu samples spiked with urea.

head.ce:title: Ionic liquid-enhanced lemon biomass carbon dots with sustainable use in bionic antibody microspheres for urea capture and ethyl carbamate inhibition

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Technology and Business University, 11 Fucheng Road, Beijing 100048, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Beijing Technology and Business University, 11 Fucheng Road, Beijing 100048, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we reported the room-temperature fabrication of ionic liquid-modified carbon dots encapsulated in bionic antibodies (IL-modified CDs@BAs) by one-pot green synthesis. In order to enhance the fluorescence intensity of CDs, imidazole ILs and lemon rich in heteroatoms were selected as CDs modifiers and sources. The resulting IL-modified CDs@BAs showed good selectivity and capture toward urea and obviously induced fluorescence quenching by template-binding. The inhibition rate ofIL-modified CDs@BAs on the urea pathway of ethyl carbamate was about 29.07% in the simulated Huangjiu system, indicating a good inhibitory effect. The IL-modified CDs@BAs system was also reproducible after five consecutive uses, thus reducing the economic cost. This research would expand the application fields of BAs-based optical sensing system from the perspectives of energy conservation, environmental protection and resource recovery, focusing on their application in the field of food safety control.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), one of the most widely studied carbon-based materials, were considered to have low cytotoxicity, high biocompatibility, and low complex aqueous synthesis (Ding, Yu, Wei, & Xiong, 2016; Sun & Lei, 2017). Similar to traditional semiconductor quantum dots, CDs had excellent luminescence properties, and its emission could be adjusted according to size, surface structure and excitation wavelength (Baker & Baker, 2010; Liu, Ye, & Mao, 2007). CDs had advantages in water solubility, functionalization, photobleaching resistance, toxicity and biocompatibility, and had a wider range of photoluminescence profiles, which were superior to quantum dots (H. Li, Kang, Liu, & Lee, 2012; Li et al., 2010; Zhang, Zhu, Li, Liu, & Sun, 2022). In recent years, with the increasingly prominent energy crisis, the use of cheap and renewable biomass in nature to produce sustainable use and functional biomass has attracted increasing attention of scientific researchers. Most biomass CDs were prepared from agricultural, forestry or food wastes, including peanut bran (An et al., 2022), sugar cane bagasse (Chai, He, Fan, Kang, & Song, 2019), rice husk(Chaudhary, Kumar, Kaur, & Mehta, 2016), wheat straw (Yuan et al., 2015), watermelon peel (Zhou, Sheng, Han, Zou, & Li, 2012), mango peel (Jiao et al., 2019), lychee (Sahoo et al., 2020), prawn shell (H. Zhang, Kang, Wang, Zhang, & Zhao, 2016), coffee grounds (Wang et al., 2016a), wool (Wang et al., 2016b). In addition to the advantages of energy saving, biomass CDs also had good fluorescence quantum yield, which might be the results of the abundance of heteroatoms in biomass. In particular, N and S provided an effective way to enhance their fluorescence properties, and adjust their electronic and chemical properties (Liu, Tian, Wang, Zhang, & Qin, 2012).

Ionic liquids (ILs), a green reactive medium, have been used for sensitization of fluorescence protein, quantum dots, and the lanthanide fluorescence (Devi & Maji, 2011; Han, Ryan, Rosado, Drummond, & Greaves, 2021; Jiang, Yuan, Zhang, Liu, & Sun, 2020) due to the non-volatility, high ionic density, stability, and ionic conductivity. Devi et al. first reported benzoate anion ILs as a ligand sensitized the fluorescence enhancement of lanthanide compounds and reduced non-radiative channels under non-aqueous conditions. Our group has studied the fluorescence sensitization and stabilization of ILs for a long time, and further explained the relevant fluorescence mechanism. We confirmed the fluorescence sensitization of heteroatom co-doped quantum dots by ILs (Jiang et al., 2020), prepared IL-sensitized CDs (Zhang, Tang, & Liu, 2019) and IL-stabilized S-doped CDs (Zhang, Wang, Xie, Geng, & Liu, 2020). The enhancement of fluorescence and luminescence mechanism of ILs on heteroatom doped biomass CDs hasn't been reported.

Bionic antibodies (BAs), also known as artificial antibodies, were biomimetic synthetic nano-polymers that could selectively attach to target molecules through a “lock and key” mechanism similar to biological systems (BelBruno, 2019). Thanks to this unique preparation process, BAs had good rigid structure, unique three-dimensional hole identification, and sustainable use. This has led to the development of new analytical methods to effectively detect trace substances, because they were stable over a wide range of pH values, solvents and temperatures. In recent years, more and more researchers have focused on the high selectivity of BAs coupled with fluorescence CDs, which were used for rapid sensing detection (Hu et al., 2021; Shabnam & Hajar, 2021; Shirani, Rezaei, Ensafi, & Ramezani, 2021) and imaging applications. In general, there were relatively few reports on separation and removal applications based on BAs and CDs coupling. In my opinion, the key scientific issues for the separation and removal of hazardous substances were the efficient recognition ability of BAs, the high fluorescence response of CDs, green and easy to assemble, so as to realize the visualization and accurate control of hazards.

Ethyl carbamate (EC), a 2A carcinogen, might be an important limiting factor restricting the development of the alcoholic beverage industry (Jiao, Dong, & Chen, 2014). How to control the formation of EC was clearly the focus of current research. Many reports have attempted to inhibit EC in fermented alcoholic beverage by physically changing raw materials and process conditions, adding exogenous chemical inhibitors, and screening superior microbial strains. However, there was still no very satisfactory method to inhibit EC unanimously. Therefore, we developed a method to control EC by blocking the formation of the precursor using the targeted adsorption of BAs. The EC precursor contained urea, citrulline, cyanide, and carbamyl phosphate, but urea was the primary pathway for the production of EC (Dayeon, Bogyoung, Eunjoo, Seri, & Hwijin, 2015; Gowd, Su, Karlovsky, & Chen, 2018; Ma, Guo, & Zhang, 2021; Zhao et al., 2013). Therefore, we chose urea as the target.

In the study, we first demonstrated a green, simple, and economical one-step hydrothermal method for preparing lemon derivative CDs. As a readily available, widely distributed and renewable biomass, lemon contains a large number of hydrocarbons necessary for CDs preparation. In order to enhance the fluorescence intensity of CDs, imidazole ILs and biomass rich in heteroatoms were selected as CD modifier and sources, respectively. Then, the system of CDs encapsulated into BAs (CDs@BAs) was constructed and applied to capture urea in fermented alcoholic beverages. The excellent advantage of this system was real-time, on-site, rapid, and ultra-sensitive identification for the urea capture. The inhibitory effect of urea pathway on EC in simulated Huangjiu was further calculated. At last, the molecular mechanism of urea capture and the specific mechanism of fluorescence enhancement were discussed. All abbreviations in the paper were presented in Table S1.

Urea, lactic acid, sodium sulfate anhydrous, ethyl ether and n-hexane were obtained from Fuchen Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). 1-carboxymethyl-3-methylimidazolium chloride, tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), ammonium hydroxide solution (NH3·H2O), thiourea, methylurea, hydroxyurea and formamide were provided by Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Carboxyethylsilanetriol sodium salt (25 wt% solution in H2O, CEST) was bought by J&K Scientific Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). All other reagents and solvents were of at least analytical grade and purchased from Beijing Chemicals (Beijing, China). Lemons and two Huangjiu samples were purchased from Yonghui superstores (Beijing, China). Ultra-pure water (A.S. Watson, Beijing, China) was used throughout the whole experimental process.

Scanning electron microscope (SEM; SU 8020, Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM; FEI Tecnai G2F30, FEI, USA) were used to take the images and observe the morphologies of prepared LBCDs and IL-modified CDs@BAs. Their surface functional groups and chemical bonds were obtained by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) (Vertex 70, Bruker, Germany) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Thermo Escalab 250Xi spectrometer, Thermo Fisher, USA). X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) images of LBCDs was recorded using a Brucker D8 advance diffractometer (Bruker, Germany). Transient fluorescence lifetime was detected by a FS5 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK). A Synergy H1 microplate reader (Biotek, Winooski, VT, USA) was used to measure the changes in fluorescence performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of lemon biomass carbon dots (LBCDs)
ce:section.ce:para: LBCDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method. Briefly, lemons were bought from online and were divided into three parts, which were lemon seed, rind and juice. Firstly, the lemons were cut into small pieces and separated the rinds, seeds and juice. Secondly, the parts were dried for 6 h at 60 °C and ground into powder for later use. Then, 0.4 g of lemon powder from different parts was dissolved into 40 mL of ultra-pure water, and was put into 50 mL of autoclave lined with polytetrafluoroethylene heating for 6 h at 200 ℃. The precipitates were then naturally cooled to room temperature and centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min to separate them from the mixture, and then filtered through a 0.22 μm filter. Finally, the resulting LBCDs was shaded and stored in a 4 °C refrigerator for future use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of IL-modified CDs@BAs and its control group
ce:section.ce:para: The IL-modified CDs@BAs were synthesized by sol–gel method. Briefly, in a 25 mL of round-bottom flask, 0.2 mmoL urea was dissolved in 10.0 mL of ethanol, mixed with 1 mg of ILs and 0.8 mmoL of CEST. Then, 1.0 mL of LBCDs was added and stirred for 1 h. Subsequently, 4 mmoL of TEOS and 200 μL of NH3·H2O were added into the reaction flask. After wrapping in foil, the mixture solution was stirred continuously for 23 h. The MIPs collected by centrifugation (10000 rpm, 10 min) were washed with ethanol several times until urea could not be detected by ultraviolet (UV) spectrometer. Finally, IL-modified CDs@BAs were dried at 50 °C and ground into powder for later use. As a contrast, the control group was prepared under the same conditions without adding urea.

When the optimum excitation wavelength of IL-modified CDs@BAs was determined to be 360 nm, the emission wavelength was measured in the range of 390 to 700 nm. Static adsorption experiment was carried out by the urea solution (0.01–5 μg L−1) and IL-modified CDs@BAs dispersed in ethanol (2 mg mL−1). The suspension was placed in a shaker at room temperature and shaken at a constant velocity for 1 h. After complete adsorption, the suspension was added to a 96-well plate and its fluorescence properties were measured to obtain linear slope, correlation coefficient (R 2) and fluorescence response (F 0/F). The standard Stern-Volmer quenching relationship was shown in the Eq. (1):
(1)
(
F 0
F
= 1 + K s v
[ c ]
)

In Eq. (1), F 0 and F were the fluorescence intensities of IL-modified CDs@BAs incubated with ethanol in the absence and presence of the urea template, respectively. Besides, Ksv was called quenching constant, and [c] was the concentration of urea added.

In order to study the adsorption time of dynamic experiment, IL-modified CDs@BAs suspension (2 mg mL−1) and urea standard solution (5 μg L−1) were mixed in equal volume. Then, the fluorescence intensity was measured and recorded at 0, 3, 6, 9, 12, 15, 18, 21, 24, 27, 30, 33, 36, 39, 42 and 45 min. Finally, the F0 /F values at different times were calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, interference, and competitiveness analysis of IL-modified CDs@BAs
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the selectivity of IL-modified CDs@BAs was carried out by using four substances with similar chemical structures to urea, including thiourea, methylurea, hydroxyurea and formamide. Four structure analogues with the same concentration of urea (0.01–5 μg L−1) were mixed with IL-modified CDs@BAs and the control, respectively. Then, interference experiments were conducted to verify the influence of several common cations, such as Co+, Mn2+, Ba2+, Ag+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Na+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Cd+, Al3+, to detect the sensitivity of IL-modified CDs@BAs. The same volume of urea and interfering cations were uniformly mixed with IL-modified CDs@BAs, and then placed on a 96-well plate for fluorescence measurement. Finally, in the competitive experiment, urea solution (5 μg L−1) was incubated with 5, 10 and 15 times of structural analogues.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of fluorescence properties of LBCDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Fluorescence quantum yield (QY) was usually used to evaluate the fluorescence properties of LBCDs. When the optimal fluorescence emission wavelength of LBCDs was 450 nm, quinine sulfate (QY = 56% ethanol) was selected as the reference material. QY can be calculated by Eq. (2),
(2)
Q 2
=
Q 1
×
A 1
A 2
×
I 2
I 1
×
N
2
2
N
1
2

In Eq. (2), Q2 and Q1 were the QYs of the LBCDs and quinine sulfate; A2 and A1 were absorbance of LBCDs and quinine sulfate; I2 and I1 were the integral fluorescence intensity of the LBCDs and quinine sulfate; N2 and N1 were the refractive indices of LBCDs and quinine sulfate solutions, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability and reusability analysis of LBCDs and IL-modified CDs@BAs
ce:section.ce:para: The LBCDs and IL-modified CDs@BAs were continuously irradiated in a 365 nm UV light box. The fluorescence was measured every 20 min for 2 h, and its short-term stability under UV light was observed. Besides, the fluorescence properties of LBCDs and IL-modified CDs@BAs suspensions were measured every two days under dark conditions for a month to demonstrate their long-term stability.

In order to demonstrate the reproducibility of IL-modified CDs@BAs, the following experiments were conducted. Firstly, urea standard solution (5 μg L−1) was mixed with IL-modified CDs@BAs in equal volume. After adsorption, the suspension was transferred to a 96-well plate, and the fluorescence intensity was measured at this time. Then, the urea template was extracted with ethanol by Soxhlet extraction. The adsorption-elution-adsorption process was cycled 5 times, and F 0/F was calculated for each time to evaluate its reusability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Capture of urea in real samples and inhibition of EC in simulated systems
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility of IL-modified CDs@BAs was verified by using two Huangjiu samples provided by local factories. Firstly, with different concentrations of urea (0.5, 2, and 5 μg L−1) samples will be diluted 1,000 times. In addition, the recovery rate and relative standard deviation (RSD) of urea in food samples were calculated.

In order to explore effect inhibitory effect of IL-modified CDs@BAs binding with urea on EC formation, the following simulation system was performed. Firstly, urea was selected as 10 g and 20 g, respectively, and then 800 mL of ultra-pure water, 150 mL of ethanol and 5 mL of lactic acid were added to the beaker successively. The pH was adjusted to 4.0 with 1 mol L-1 NaOH solution. Next, the above solution in the beaker was transferred to a 1000 mL volumetric bottle, and the volume was fixed with ultra-pure water. The Huangjiu samples with alcohol concentration of 15% and urea concentration of 10 mg L-1 (simulation system 1) and 20 mg L-1 (simulation system 2) were obtained.

Inhibition experiment of IL-modified CDs@BAs on EC formation was carried out through the following steps: 800 mL of simulation system 1 and 2 were transferred from volumetric bottles to beakers, divided into four equal volumes. Then, 0, 20, 50 and 100 mg of IL-modified CDs@BAs were added to the simulated system 1 and 2, respectively, heating at 85 °C for 3 h. Finally, according to GB 5009.223–2014, the EC content was detected by gas chromatograph- mass spectrometer (GC–MS), and the relationship between different contents of ILs-modified CDs@BAs and EC concentration in simulated Huangjiu was studied.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of IL-modified CDs@BAs and its urea capture mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation process of LBCDs by simple solvothermal method was shown in Fig. 1 A. Environmental-friendly and low-cost LBCDs were synthesized by the carbonization of lemon via hydrothermal process. Hydrothermal carbonization converted organic matter contained in lemon to CDs and functionalized its surface, making it water soluble and photoluminescent (Kang, Huang, Yang, Yan, & Chen, 2020). Three parts of lemons were used to optimize the raw materials for LBCDs synthesis. The highest QY of LBCDs synthesized from lemon seeds was 13.1%, with reference to quinine sulfate (QY = 56% ethanol). Therefore, in the following experiments, lemon seeds were chose as the raw material to synthesize LBCDs.

Then, IL-modified CDs@BAs was coated on the fluorescent center surface of LBCDs by a sol–gel method (Fig. 1B). Firstly, IL was added to improve the fluorescence properties of the nano-polymer during the synthesis of IL-modified CDs@BAs. Secondly, IL could also enhance the recognition and adsorption capacity of nanomaterials. In addition, CEST was used as the other functional monomer because the carboxyl group contained in CEST could be bonded with the amino functional group of urea by hydrogen bonding. The main bonding reaction of amino and carboxyl groups during the preparation of IL-modified CDs@BAs was shown in Fig. 1C. Under the catalysis of ammonia water, IL, CEST and TEOS underwent hydrolysis condensation reaction, forming a silicon oxide silicon shell layer on the surface of LBCDs. Urea in the synthesized nanomaterial were eluted by Soxhlet extraction. Due to the good solubility of urea with strongly polar ethanol, hydrogen bonds were broken and the targets were removed. A large number of exposed three-dimensional recognition cavities could be used for urea specific recognition and re-adsorption. After multiple identification and capture, the fluorescence intensity of LBCDs decreased significantly. Based on this, the causes of fluorescence quenching were further discussed.

Fig. 1D showed the UV absorption peaks of urea, LBCDs, and an equivalent mixture of urea and LBCDs. The UV absorption peak of urea was 235 nm, while that of LBCDs and their mixed solutions was 280 nm. When LBCDs were exposed to urea, the UV absorption peak at 280 nm decreased. This quenching phenomenon could be attributed to the interaction between targets and recognition components. However, the maximum UV absorption peaks of LBCDs did not change with urea addition. The maximum UV absorption wavelength was quite different from the optimal emission wavelength of LBCDs (λ Em = 450 nm). In addition, fluorescence lifetime experiment was also carried out to investigate the fluorescence decay characteristics of LBCDs in the presence and absence of the target. Fig. 1E and F showed the transient fluorescence spectra of LBCDs before and after adding quencher. The fluorescence lifetime of LBCDs was 8.62 ns. After mixing with urea, the fluorescence lifetime was 9.50 ns, which was not obvious. In conclusion, photoinduced electron transfer (PET) might be the real cause of fluorescence quenching. The quenching mechanism of PET was caused by electron transfer between CDs and urea (Zu et al., 2017). In this process, the urea quencher could combine with specific groups on the CDs surface to form a complex that could return to the ground state without emitting photons. During PET reduction, LBCDs acted as the electron acceptor to receive electrons from the electron donor. The driving force of electron transfer in reducing PET was the energy gap between the lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (LUMO) of urea and the highest occupied molecular orbitals (HOMO) of LBCDs (Luo et al., 2020).

Fig. 2 A showed the morphologies of LBCDs obtained using high-resolution TEM (HRTEM). Its specific performance was uniform distribution of small spherical. Using Nano Measurer 1.2, the mean diameter of the LBCDs was calculated to be about 4.9 nm (Fig. 2A: Inset). Fig. 2B and Fig. 2C showed SEM and TEM images of IL-modified CDs@BAs, respectively. SEM images showed that the prepared IL-modified CDs@BAs not only had a uniform core–shell spherical structure, but also had a rough surface due to the existence of some three-dimensional recognition cavities that could specifically interact with the targets. The average particle size of IL-modified CDs@BAs was calculated to be 171.8 nm, which was obviously bigger than LBCDs (Fig. 2B: Inset). TEM images exhibited that the surface of the prepared LBCDs was coated with a thick shell layer and presented a sol state. Energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) of IL-modified CDs@BAs showed the distribution of C, N, O and Si in the nano-polymer. Therefore, it could be inferred that the TEOS layer has successfully covered the surface of the LBCDs (Fig. 2D).

The oxygen-containing functional groups of LBCDs, IL-modified CDs@BAs and its control group were often characterized by FT-IR. Fig. S1A showed FT-IR spectra of the above three. For the FT-IR spectrum of the LBCDs, a large and wide absorption peak appeared at 3453 cm−1 due to the stretching vibration of a OH bond, which made LBCDs hydrophilic. In addition, the absorption band at 1636 cm−1 was attributed to a CO bond. For the FT-IR spectrum of the IL-modified CDs@BAs, the strong absorption band first observed at about 1087 cm−1 was due to asymmetric stretching of a Si-O-Si bond after silane hydrolysis. Besides, the narrow peak observed at 796 cm−1 was related to the stretching vibration of a SiOH bond. In addition to the characteristic peaks of silane, the small absorption band at 1555 cm−1 was caused by the bending motion of a NH bond. The stretching vibration peak of CO at 1647 cm−1 indicated that the carboxyl group (–COOH) contained in CEST was successfully added to the surface of the IL-modified CDs@BAs. Finally, the formation of a small absorption band at 3448 cm−1 was attributed to a OH bond of LBCDs. These results suggested that the silicon shell has covered the surface of LBCDs and the preparation of IL-modified CDs@BAs was successful. Notably, the locations and appearances of the major bands of IL-modified CDs@BAs were very similar to the control group, indicating that urea was removed during elution.

XPS data from LBCDs and IL-modified CDs@BAs further confirmed the FT-IR results. The XPS full-spectrum scanning image of LBCDs showed that a weak peak at 400 eV, and two strong peaks at 285 eV and 532 eV, which were associated with N, C, and O elements, respectively (Fig. S1B). Fig. S1C, 1D and 1E exhibited the peak fitting results of N 1S, C 1s, and O 1s, respectively. On this basis, it could be deduced that LBCDs were N-doped CDs (N-CDs). According to XPS data analysis, the percentage of N 1s in LBCDs accounted for 11.45% of the total number of atoms. XPS full-spectrum scanning image of IL-modified CDs@BAs revealed a strong peak at 532 eV and three much smaller peaks at 103, 285 and 500 eV corresponding to O 1s, Si 2p, C 1s and N 1s, respectively (Fig. S2A). Three characteristic peaks at 284.7, 286.2, and 288.1 eV in the peak fitting results of C 1s were attributed to CC, CO, and CO bonds (Fig. S2B). The four characteristic peaks at 530.6, 531.9, 532.0 and 533.0 eV corresponded to the CO, Si-O, CO and CO bonds fitted by O 1s peak fitting results, respectively (Fig. S2C). As the content of N 1s was extremely low, its peak fitting result was not obvious (Fig. S2D). Since the elements in LBCDs included C, N and O, the presence of Si also confirmed by the XPS spectrum of IL-modified CDs@BAs (Fig. S2E). Therefore, this result further proved the successful preparation of IL-modified CDs@BAs.

The crystal structure of LBCDs and IL-modified CDs@BAs were characterized by XRD. The XRD patterns of the LBCDs and IL-modified CDs@BAs in Fig. S2F and G showed that both of them had a broad (002) diffraction peak at about 2θ = 24◦. However, IL-modified CDs@BAs synthesized by sol–gel method had sharper diffraction peaks, which indicated that nanomaterials had a better crystal structure (Zuo, Lu, Sun, Guo, & He, 2016).

In the polymerization process of IL-modified CDs@BAs, the solvent could not only dissolve the polymerization reagents, but also provided a porous structure for IL-modified CDs@BAs, thereby improving the binding rate of imprinted molecules and functional monomers during recognition process. The solvent was optimized for IL-modified CDs@BAs polymer due to its greater effect on specific adsorption and selectivity. The IL-modified CDs@BAs was uniformly dispersed in water, methanol, ethanol and acetonitrile, to compare the fluorescence intensity. IL-modified CDs@BAs dissolved in ethanol had the highest fluorescence intensity compared to other solvents (Fig. S3A). The reason might be that in the preparation of IL-modified CDs@BAs containing non-covalent bonds, polar protic solvents such as ethanol had little effect on hydrogen bonds in the bonding reaction, thus weakening the interaction between the solvent and each component. This also led to an enhanced bond interaction between urea and CEST, which further enhanced the recognition ability of IL-modified CDs@BAs. Hence, among the solvents studied, ethanol might be the best medium for IL-modified CDs@BAs.

On the basis of the optimal solvent, the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of IL-modified CDs@BAs were further optimized. IL-modified CDs@BAs was uniformly dispersed in ethanol, and its fluorescence properties were recorded near the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of LBCDs. The excitation/emission wavelength was eventually set at 360/450 nm (Fig. S3B).

Finally, in order to maximize the recognition and capture of urea by IL-modified CDs@BAs, the molar ratios of urea, CEST and TEOS were optimized. The fluorescence intensity, fluorescence response and quenching constant at different ratios were shown in C, 3D and 3E. It could be seen that when the molar ratio of the three components increased from 1: 4: 12 to 1: 4: 20, the fluorescence intensity and quenching constant of each ratio increased, respectively. This indicated that under certain conditions, the specific selectivity of IL-modified CDs@BAs to the target molecules was enhanced. At the same time, IL-modified CDs@BAs required a certain amount of cross-linking to form a rigid skeleton that maintained its porous structure and specific adsorption sites. However, when the molar ratio of the three components was increased to 1: 4: 24, the data for each ratio exhibited varying degrees of reduction. There might be two reasons for this result. First of all, excessive CEST might adversely affect the performance of the IL-modified CDs@BAs in selecting urea. In addition, excessive TEOS might form a thick shell structure on the IL-modified CDs@BAs surface, hindering urea specific recognition and capture. Therefore, the optimal molar ratio of urea, CEST, and TEOS was selected as 1: 4: 20 for the subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of ILs on IL-modified CDs@ BAs and fluorescent detection kinetics
ce:section.ce:para: The ILs contained abundant positively charged imidazole cationic groups. It not only could be used as the functional monomer to form uniform microspheres with CEST through hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interaction, but also significantly improved the fluorescence intensity by supplementing electrons. The preparation of microsphere was greatly affected by the amount of ILs. Different amounts of ILs (0, 0.5, 1, and 2 mg) were optimized during the synthesis process to further enhance fluorescence and improve the capture capacity of urea. A exhibited the effect of different amounts of ILs on fluorescence intensity of IL-modified CDs@BAs. When the dosage of ILs was 1 mg, the fluorescence intensity of the prepared IL-modified CDs@BAs was about 1.8 times than that of control group. Fig. S4B showed the fluorescence quenching effect of IL-modified CDs@BAs by adding urea solutions with different concentrations. The addition of 1 mg IL-modified CDs@BAs showed the best fluorescence response to different concentrations of urea solution. Fig. S4C compared the effect of different amounts of ILs on the fluorescence quenching constant. The quenching constant was first obtained by linear fitting of Eq. (1). When the amount of ILs was less than 1 mg, the quenching constant was directly proportional to the amount of ILs. When the dosage of ILs was 1 mg, the quenching constant reached the maximum value, about 0.0573. The reason for this might be that ILs increased the fluorescence intensity of IL-modified CDs@BAs, thus improving its fluorescence properties and response sensitivity. When the addition of ILs was greater than 1 mg, the quenching constant decreased. The reason might be that the specific recognition sites of IL-modified CDs@BAs were blocked due to excessive accumulation or even aggregation of ILs in the synthesis process, leading to the weakening of its fluorescence performance. Therefore, 1 mg of ILs was selected as the optimal dosage for subsequent experiments.

The adsorption time of urea on IL-modified CDs@BAs was measured by adsorption equilibrium. Kinetic adsorption time played a pivotal role in the experimental results, which not only reflected the adsorption rate of IL-modified CDs@BAs on the target molecule, but also determined the vibration time of subsequent experiments. The F 0/F value of IL-modified CDs@BAs increased significantly in the first 36 min, and then gradually stabilized (Fig. S4D). After 36 min, the F 0/F value stabilized at about 1.49. Therefore, 36 min was selected as the optimal adsorption equilibrium time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response-concentration dependence for IL-modified CDs@BAs for urea
ce:section.ce:para: Under the above optimal conditions, IL-modified CDs@BAs was used as the fluorescence sensing materials to adsorb urea. Fig. 3 A and B showed fluorescence quenching curves for IL-modified CDs@BAs and its control group at concentrations ranging from 0.01 to 5 μg L−1. The fluorescence intensities of IL-modified CDs@BAs and its control group decreased with the increasing of urea concentration, which confirmed the quenching effect of urea on their fluorescence from the side. The fluorescence quenching property was a significant basis for evaluating the selectivity and sensitivity of IL-modified CDs@BAs. As for IL-modified CDs@BAs, the linear range (0.01–5 μg L−1) showed F 0/F = 1.027 + 0.0573Curea, R 2 was 0.979 (Fig. 3C). However, the fluorescence quenching effect of the control group was not obvious (Fig. 3D). In this case, F 0/F = 1.088 + 0.0243Curea, and R 2 was 0.987. According to the quenching constant, the fluorescence quenching efficiency of IL-modified CDs@BAs was obviously higher than its control group. The limit of detection (LOD) of IL-modified CDs@BAs for urea was calculated to be 1.6 ng L-1 using the signal-to-noise (S/N) = 3 (Zhang, Xu, Liu, & Sun, 2022). The main reason for the above results was that there were significant differences in the spatial structure of the two polymers. Specifically, IL-modified CDs@BAs had excellent adsorption capacity for urea, mainly due to the formation of three-dimensional recognition cavities that complemented the structure and space of the target. Therefore, the formed three-dimensional cavity had a memory function for the template molecules, resulting in a stronger binding interaction with urea.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, competitiveness, and interference analysis of IL-modified CDs@BAs
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity, anti-interference and competitiveness were three vital factors to evaluate the functional characteristics of IL-modified CDs@BAs. First of all, the selectivity of IL-modified CDs@ BAs was investigated in the concentrations of 0.01–5 μg L−1 using structural and functional analogues of urea, including thiourea, methylourea, hydroxyurea and formamide. As can be seen from the Fig. 4 A, the F 0/F value of IL-modified CDs@ BAs for urea constructed in this experiment was significantly higher than that of other analogues. To further verify the above conclusions, the quenching constants of IL-modified CDs@BAs and control group for target molecule and its analogues were obtained by Eq. (1) (Fig. 4B). A number of specific recognition capture sites have been generated in the preparation of IL-modified CDs@BAs that were highly compatible with the target substance in terms of size, structure, or functional groups. Therefore, the quenching effect of IL-modified CDs@BAs on urea was much better than that of its analogues. On the contrary, the quenching constant of urea in the control group was roughly the same as that of its analogues, because the synthetic material itself did not have the ability to recognize the target molecule.

Then, common cations with a concentration of 2 mM such as Co+, Mn2+, Ba2+, Ag+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Na+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Cd+, Al3+ were used to prove the anti-interference ability of IL-modified CDs@BAs and urea in the presence of potential interfering ions. Obviously, the above potential interfering ions had little effect on the ability of the mixed system to recognize and capture urea (Fig. 4C).

In addition, in the system with high concentration of analogues, the urea recognition sites on the surface of the constructed IL-modified CDs@BAs, was destroyed, resulting in the instability of F 0/F value. Four kinds of analogues, whose concentrations were increased by 5, 10 and 15 times higher than urea respectively, were mixed with urea solution with concentration of 5 μg L−1 in equal volume to compare their changes of fluorescence intensity (Fig. 4D). The result showed that there is no significant change in the F 0/F value compared with the solution containing only the target substance. This demonstrated that IL-modified CDs@BAs nanomaterials had high selectivity to urea through physical and chemical combination.

The effects of UV irradiation and storage on the fluorescence stability of LBCDs and IL-modified CDs@BAs were studied in a 365 nm UV light box at room temperature. LBCDs and IL-modified CDs@ BAs were well tolerant to persistent UV light (365 nm, 120 min). The fluorescence intensity varied little at each time point (Fig. S5A, B). In addition, the fluorescence intensity of LBCDs and IL-modified CDs@BAs was measured every two days for a month under dark conditions (Fig. S5C, D). The results showed that the LBCDs and IL-modified CDs@BAs constructed by fluorescence method had excellent detection stability and photobleaching resistance.

The reproducibility of IL-modified CDs@ BAs was studied by measuring the F 0/F values of five cycles after adsorption and elution. As shown in Fig. S6, after five consecutive adsorption and elution cycles, the F0/F values of nanomaterials was 96.8% of the initial value, with RSD = 1.2%. These results indicated that IL-modified CDs@BAs could be reused in urea determination and had good stability, which was of great significance for future practical application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Capture of urea in real samples and inhibition of EC in simulated systems
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the practical application of IL-modified CDs@BAs in fermented beverages, two different kinds of Huangjiu were selected for testing. It should be noted that urea was not detected in Huangjiu without adding urea standard solution. Next, the Huangjiu was incubated with 0.5, 2, and 5 μg L−1 of urea solution, respectively. As shown in Table 1 , IL-modified CDs@BAs showed an effective recovery (93.57–108.63%, RSD less than 1.61%; 92.46–108.79%, RSD＜1.99%). The above results indicated that IL-modified CDs@BAs could identify and capture urea in real Huangjiu.

Precursors such as urea could produce EC in fermented beverages through arginine deaminase pathway and/or cyanogenic degradation pathway. As mentioned in 3.1, the ILs-modified CDs@BAs surface had many three-dimensional recognition cavities that could specifically recognize and capture urea. After the addition of IL-modified CDs@BAs, the urea content in simulated Huangjiu decreased, and the concentration of EC also decreased accordingly. Thus, the desire to indirectly inhibit EC concentration was realized by adding IL-modified CDs@BAs to bind with urea. Fig. 5 A showed the GC–MS chromatogram of the relative strength of EC in simulation system 2 with 0 or 100 mg IL-modified CDs@BAs, respectively. Three different contents of IL-modified CDs@BAs (20, 50 and 100 mg) were added into simulation system 1 and 2, respectively. A free of IL-modified CDs@BAs sample was used as a blank. As shown in Fig. 5B, EC concentration decreased with the increase of IL-modified CDs@BAs content compared with blank. In simulation system 1, the EC concentrations were 90.24, 86.31 and 75.24 mg L-1 when IL-modified CDs@BAs was added to 20, 50 and 100 mg, respectively. The inhibition rates of EC concentrations were 8.11%, 12.10%, and 29.07%. Similarly, the inhibition rates in simulation system 2 were 5.94%, 9.43% and 19.94%, respectively. The results showed that IL-modified CDs@BAs could indirectly inhibit EC formation by combining with urea (Fig. 5C).

Finally, Table S2 summarized some literatures related to urea capture and adsorption in the last five years. The methods involved colorimetric, fluorometric and electrochemical sensors. Compared with the literatures listed in the Table S2, the method constructed in this experiment had the advantages of suitable linear range and low LOD. Therefore, IL-modified CDs@BAs was a potential and excellent method for urea capture and adsorption.

In summary, we synthesized biomass CDs from lemon seed, lemon juice and lemon peel by one-step hydrothermal method under mild conditions, and compared their fluorescence intensity. The results showed that the CDs prepared from lemon seed had the highest fluorescence QY of 13.1%. Further, the ILs modified CDs were encapsulated into IL-modified CDs@BAs to form an optical capture system of specific urea. A linear range from 0.01 to 5 μg L−1 with LOD of 1.6 ng L-1 exhibited an ultra-sensitive optical adsorption. The results of spiked recovery showed that the recognition rates of urea in this system were 92.46–108.79 %, indicating a good selectivity for urea. The inhibition rate of EC by urea route was 29.07% by using the IL-modified CDs@BAs system. This was a unique example of IL-modified CDs@BAs system not only for removing hazards from food samples but also for sustainable and continuous removal of hazards. After continuous use for 5 times, the adsorption effect was still excellent (RSD 1.9%). This provides a novel idea for the construction and application of optical sensing system on IL-modified CDs@BAs.

Chen Meng: Methodology, Validation, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Chenchen Xie: Methodology. Jingbo He: Data curation. Xiaolin Chen: Writing – review & editing. Huilin Liu: Conceptualization, Validation, Resources, Supervision. Baoguo Sun: Conceptualization, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 32072335), the Cultivation Project of Double First-Class Disciplines of Food Science and Engineering, Beijing Technology & Business University (BTBU).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2023.135715.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
